_stage: CPAL Stage 1 — Wrong-Model Identification + LP Level Classification

_concept_meta.java_concept: Reference equality. Error: == returns false for two Strings with identical content.

_input_recap.student_message: I know I should use .equals() for strings, that's what the textbook says. But I don't really get it — they're both 'hello' so why is == false? I printed them both and they look identical. Something about how strings are stored?
diagnostic_phrases_extracted: they're both 'hello' so why is == false
I printed them both and they look identical
Something about how strings are stored?

wrong_model_identification.wrong_model_description: == compares the content of strings. Two strings with the same characters are equal under ==.
wrong_model_identification.wrong_model_origin: Python: == calls __eq__ which compares content. Mathematics: equality means same value.
wrong_model_identification.matched_signal: they're both hello so why is == false
wrong_model_identification.match_evidence: Direct verbatim match — Marcus's phrasing is exactly the documented signal. He also produces the second SE-A signal almost verbatim ('I printed them both and they look identical' = catalogue's 'I printed them both and they look identical').

alternatives_considered.wrong_belief_short: new String() makes a copy of the literal; copy and original are the same thing
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: Code uses new String() and the wrong belief overlaps, but Marcus's phrasing focuses on what == does ('why is == false'), not on what new does. SE-A is the more specific match.
Marcus already accepts they are NOT interchangeable ('I know I should use .equals()'). SE-C is held by students who don't yet differentiate.

lp_signals_detected.evidence: 'I know I should use .equals() for strings, that's what the textbook says'

lp_signals_detected.signal: Cannot explain the mechanism — gestures vaguely at storage
lp_signals_detected.evidence: 'Something about how strings are stored?' — explicit hedge, the L2 catalogue example
lp_signals_detected.rubric_match: L2: 'Cannot explain why == fails. Hedges: something about how strings are stored?'

lp_signals_detected.signal: Has not yet reached L3 — no model of references vs heap addresses
lp_signals_detected.evidence: No mention of memory address, reference, allocation, or heap
lp_signals_detected.rubric_match: Below L3: L3 requires 'Variable holds a reference (memory address). == compares addresses.'

plateau_gate._note: First session at L2 on this concept. Plateau would only fire on the second consecutive L2 session without progress.

expert_benchmark_key_ideas: A String variable is not the string itself — it holds a memory address (a reference) pointing at a String object on the heap.
new String("hello") allocates a *new* heap object every time. Two new String("hello") calls produce two different addresses.
== compares the contents of the variables themselves: the addresses. Two different addresses → false, even if the content at those addresses is identical.
.equals() (defined on String) follows both addresses, fetches the character arrays, and compares those character-by-character → true when content matches.

downstream_effects.stage_2_intervention_filter: Eligible interventions for L2→L3 on string_equality: trace_scaffold (memory diagram walkthrough), code_animation (visualize address comparison), reference_vs_value_minimal_pair. Exclude rule_reminder (he already knows the rule).

stage_3_prompt_sections.LP-1_current_understanding: Student knows .equals() is the answer but treats it as a memorized rule, not a derivable consequence
stage_3_prompt_sections.LP-2_wrong_model_context: SE-A (Python/math equality intuition). LLM should NOT shame the rule-following — the gap is mechanism, not rule.
stage_3_prompt_sections.LP-3_target_understanding: By end of session: student can hand-trace a == b for two new String() objects, drawing two boxes (variables) holding two different arrows (addresses) pointing at two heap objects with identical content, and explain why == compares the arrows and .equals() follows them.
